content:
# Exodus Chapter 37 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 108–109; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 80–81 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_037_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_037_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_037_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_037_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Ark of the Testimony (vv. 1–9)

**1** And Bezalel [**Betsal'el**] made [**vayya'as**] the ark [**et-ha'aron**] of acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**]: two cubits [**ammatayim**] and a half [**vachetsi**] its length [**orkho**], and a cubit [**ve'ammah**] and a half [**vachetsi**] its width [**rochbo**], and a cubit [**ve'ammah**] and a half [**vachetsi**] its height [**qomato**].

**2** And he overlaid it [**vayetsappehu**] with pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**] from inside [**mibbayyit**] and from outside [**umichutz**], and he made [**vayya'as**] for it [**lo**] a rim [**zer**] of gold [**zahav**] all around [**saviv**].

**3** And he cast [**vayyitsoq**] for it [**lo**] four [**arba**] rings [**tabb'ot**] of gold [**zahav**] on [**al**] its four [**arba**] feet [**pa'amotav**]: and two [**ushtey**] rings [**tabba'ot**] on [**al**] its one [**tsal'o**] side [**ha'echat**] and two [**ushtey**] rings [**tabba'ot**] on [**al**] its other [**tsal'o**] side [**hasshenit**].

**4** And he made [**vayya'as**] poles [**baddey**] of acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**] and overlaid [**vayetsap**] them [**otam**] with gold [**zahav**].

**5** And he put [**vayyave**] the poles [**et-habbaddim**] into the rings [**battabba'ot**] on the sides [**al tsal'ot**] of the ark [**ha'aron**] to carry [**laset**] the ark [**et-ha'aron**].

**6** And he made [**vayya'as**] a mercy seat [**kapporet**] of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**]: two cubits [**ammatayim**] and a half [**vachetsi**] its length [**orkah**] and a cubit [**ve'ammah**] and a half [**vachetsi**] its width [**rochbah**].

**7** And he made [**vayya'as**] two [**shneyim**] cherubim [**keruvim**] of gold [**zahav**]; of hammered work [**miqshah**] he made [**asah**] them [**otam**], from the two [**mishshney**] ends of [**qetsot**] the mercy seat [**hakkapporet**]:

**8** one [**keruv-echad**] cherub [**keruv**] from this [**miqatseh**] end [**mizzeh**] and one [**ukeruv-echad**] cherub [**keruv**] from that [**miqatseh**] end [**mizzeh**]; from [**min**] the mercy seat [**hakkapporet**] he made [**asah**] the cherubim [**et-hakkeruvim**] from its two [**mishshney**] ends [**qetsotav**].

**9** And the cherubim [**vayyihyu hakkeruvim**] were spreading [**porsey**] their wings [**khenafayim**] upward [**lema'lah**], covering [**sokhekhim**] with their wings [**bekhanfeyhem**] over [**al**] the mercy seat [**hakkapporet**], and their faces [**ufeneyhem**] each toward the other [**ish el-achiv**]; toward [**el**] the mercy seat [**hakkapporet**] were [**hayu**] the faces of [**peney**] the cherubim [**hakkeruvim**].

---

## The Table of the Bread of the Presence (vv. 10–16)

**10** And he made [**vayya'as**] the table [**et-hashulchan**] of acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**]: two cubits [**ammatayim**] its length [**orkho**] and a cubit [**ve'ammah**] its width [**rochbo**] and a cubit [**ve'ammah**] and a half [**vachetsi**] its height [**qomato**].

**11** And he overlaid it [**vayetsap oto**] with pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**] and made [**vayya'as**] for it [**lo**] a rim [**zer**] of gold [**zahav**] all around [**saviv**].

**12** And he made [**vayya'as**] for it [**lo**] a frame [**misgeret**] of a handbreadth [**tofach**] all around [**saviv**], and he made [**vayya'as**] a rim [**zer**] of gold [**zahav**] for its frame [**lemisgaretto**] all around [**saviv**].

**13** And he cast [**vayyitsoq**] for it [**lo**] four [**arba**] rings [**tabb'ot**] of gold [**zahav**], and he put [**vayyitten**] the rings [**et-hattabba'ot**] on [**al**] the four [**arba**] corners [**happi'ot**] which [**asher**] were at its four [**le'arba**] legs [**raglav**].

**14** Close to [**le'ummat**] the frame [**hammisgeret**] were [**hayu**] the rings [**hattabba'ot**] — holders [**batim**] for the poles [**labaddim**] to carry [**laset**] the table [**et-hashulchan**].

**15** And he made [**vayya'as**] the poles [**et-habbaddim**] of acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**] and overlaid [**vayetsap**] them [**otam**] with gold [**zahav**], to carry [**laset**] the table [**et-hashulchan**].

**16** And he made [**vayya'as**] the vessels [**et-hakkelim**] which [**asher**] were upon [**al**] the table [**hashulchan**]: its plates [**et-qe'arotav**] and its ladles [**ve'et-kappotav**] and its pitchers [**ve'et-qesotav**] and its bowls [**ve'et-menaqiyyotav**] with which [**asher**] it would be poured out [**yussakh**] — of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**].

---

## The Lampstand of Pure Gold (vv. 17–24)

**17** And he made [**vayya'as**] the lampstand [**et-hammenorah**] of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**]; of hammered work [**miqshah**] he made [**asah**] the lampstand [**et-hammenorah**] — its base [**yerekhah**] and its shaft [**veqanah**], its cups [**gevi'eha**], its buds [**kaftoreha**] and its blossoms [**ufracheha**] — from it [**mimmennah**] they were [**hayu**].

**18** And six [**veshishah**] branches [**qanim**] going out [**yotse'im**] from its sides [**mitsiddeyha**]: three [**shloshah**] branches [**qeney**] of the lampstand [**menorah**] from its one [**mitsiddah**] side [**ha'echad**] and three [**ushloshah**] branches [**qeney**] of the lampstand [**menorah**] from its other [**mitsiddah**] side [**hassheni**].

**19** Three [**shloshah**] cups [**gevi'im**] shaped like almond blossoms [**meshukqadim**] on the one [**baqqaneh ha'echad**] branch [**baqqaneh**], a bud [**kaftor**] and a blossom [**vaferach**]; and three [**ushloshah**] cups [**gevi'im**] shaped like almond blossoms [**meshukqadim**] on the other [**baqqaneh ha'echad**] branch [**baqqaneh**], a bud [**kaftor**] and a blossom [**vaferach**] — so [**ken**] for the six [**lesheshet**] branches [**haqqanim**] going out [**hayyotse'im**] from [**min**] the lampstand [**hammenorah**].

**20** And on the lampstand [**ubammenorah**] were four [**arba'ah**] cups [**gevi'im**] shaped like almond blossoms [**meshukqadim**], its buds [**kaftoreha**] and its blossoms [**ufracheha**].

**21** And a bud [**vekaftor**] under [**tachat**] the two [**shney**] branches [**haqqanim**] from it [**mimmennu**], and a bud [**vekaftor**] under [**tachat**] the two [**shney**] branches [**haqqanim**] from it [**mimmennu**], and a bud [**vekaftor**] under [**tachat**] the two [**shney**] branches [**haqqanim**] from it [**mimmennu**] — for the six [**lesheshet**] branches [**haqqanim**] going out [**hayyotse'im**] from it [**mimmennah**].

**22** Their buds [**kaftoreyhem**] and their branches [**uqenotam**] were [**hayu**] from it [**mimmennah**] — all of it [**kullahh**] one [**echad**] hammered piece [**miqshah**] of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**].

**23** And he made [**vayya'as**] its lamps [**et-neroteyha**] seven [**shiv'ah**], and its tongs [**umalqacheyha**] and its fire pans [**umachtoteyha**] of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**].

**24** Of a talent [**kikkar**] of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**] he made [**asah**] it [**otah**] and [**ve'et**] all [**kol**] its vessels [**keleyha**].

---

## The Altar of Incense (vv. 25–28)

**25** And he made [**vayya'as**] the altar of [**et-mizbach**] incense [**haqqetoret**] of acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**]: a cubit [**ammah**] its length [**orkho**] and a cubit [**ve'ammah**] its width [**rochbo**] — square [**ravua**] — and two cubits [**ve'ammatayim**] its height [**qomato**]; from it [**mimmennu**] were [**hayu**] its horns [**qarnotav**].

**26** And he overlaid it [**vayetsap oto**] with pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**] — its top [**et-gaggo**] and its walls [**ve'et-qirotav**] all around [**saviv**] and its horns [**ve'et-qarnotav**]; and he made [**vayya'as**] for it [**lo**] a rim [**zer**] of gold [**zahav**] all around [**saviv**].

**27** And two [**ushtey**] rings [**tabba'ot**] of gold [**zahav**] he made [**asah**] for it [**lo**] beneath [**mittachat**] its rim [**lezero**], on [**al**] its two [**shney**] sides [**tsalla'ov**], on its two [**al shney**] flanks [**tsiddav**], as holders [**lebattim**] for poles [**lebaddim**] to carry it [**laset oto**] with them [**bahem**].

**28** And he made [**vayya'as**] the poles [**et-habbaddim**] of acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**] and overlaid [**vayetsap**] them [**otam**] with gold [**zahav**].

---

## The Anointing Oil and the Incense (v. 29)

**29** And he made [**vayya'as**] the holy [**et-shemen hammishchah qodesh**] anointing [**hammishchah**] oil [**shemen**] and the pure [**ve'et-qetoret**] fragrant [**hassammim**] incense [**qetoret**] — the work of [**ma'aseh**] a perfumer [**roqeach**].

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

1. **Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 108–109:** The text of Exodus 37 begins on folio 108 (page 1 image), continuing directly from the final verse of chapter 36 (the entrance screen). The three-column format is maintained throughout. The chapter opens with "vayya'as Betsal'el et-ha'aron" — the first explicit naming of Bezalel as the sole craftsman of the Ark, a distinction not found in the instructions of chapter 25, where the plural "ve'asu" (let them make) is used. The Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks are fully preserved. The chapter continues through the left column of folio 108 and onto folio 109 (page 2 image), where the lampstand description and incense altar construction complete the chapter. A masorah parva note appears adjacent to the term "miqshah" (hammered work) in v. 17, consistent with Masoretic attention to this technical term. The plene and defective spellings are consistent with scribal practice throughout the Leningrad Codex: "qomato" (its height) is written defective. The chapter is notably shorter than its instruction parallel (25:10–40 + 30:1–10, 22–38), omitting the command framework and presenting only the construction account in third-person narrative.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 80–81:** The Vaticanus Greek text of Exodus 37 presents the well-known divergence between the LXX and the MT in the Tabernacle construction narrative. In the LXX arrangement, the material corresponding to MT Exodus 37 (the Ark, table, lampstand, and incense altar) appears in a different sequence from the MT. The Vaticanus manuscript on page 80 (marked CAP. XXVII at the top right) shows the continuation of construction material in three-column uncial scriptio continua. The text visible includes references to the kiboton (Ark), the hilasterion (mercy seat), and the cherubim — the LXX equivalents of the MT's aron, kapporet, and keruvim. On page 81, the text continues with the construction of the luchnia (lampstand) and the thusiasterion (incense altar). The LXX chapter numbering diverges from the MT throughout this section: what the MT numbers as Exodus 37 corresponds to portions of LXX Exodus 37–38, reflecting the systematic rearrangement of the fulfillment narrative in the Greek tradition. The Vaticanus text preserves the standard LXX renderings: "kiboton" for "aron" (Ark), "hilasterion" for "kapporet" (mercy seat), "trapezan" for "shulchan" (table), "luchnia" for "menorah" (lampstand), and "thusiasterion tou thumimatos" for "mizbach haqqetoret" (altar of incense). The scriptio continua is clearly legible across all three columns on both pages.

### Key Translation Decisions

3. **v. 1 — "vayya'as Betsal'el et-ha'aron" (And Bezalel made the ark):** This verse is the only place in the construction narrative where a specific artisan is named as the maker of a particular object. In all other construction verses (36:8–38; 37:10–29; 38:1–20), the anonymous "vayya'as" (and he made) is used without naming the subject. The MT's singling out of Bezalel for the Ark carries theological weight: the most sacred object in the Tabernacle — the throne-footstool of YHWH's presence — is attributed to the chief artisan who was "filled with the Spirit of God" (31:3; 35:31). The LXX reads "kai epoiesen Beseleel ten kiboton" (and Bezalel made the ark-chest), preserving the named attribution. The name Bezalel (Betsal'el) means "in the shadow of God" — the man who works in God's shadow constructs the seat of God's presence.

4. **vv. 1–5 — The Ark construction paralleling 25:10–15:** The construction account follows the instruction text of 25:10–15 with near-verbatim precision. The dimensions are identical (two and a half cubits length, one and a half cubits width, one and a half cubits height). The gold overlay is applied "from inside and from outside" (mibbayyit umichutz). The four rings, the acacia poles, and their insertion into the rings are all reproduced faithfully. The only structural difference from the instruction is the shift from second-person imperative ("you shall make," "you shall overlay") to third-person narrative ("he made," "he overlaid"), signaling the transition from command to fulfillment. Notably absent from the construction account is the command of 25:15 ("the poles shall remain in the rings of the ark; they shall not be removed from it") and 25:16 ("you shall put into the ark the testimony which I shall give you") — the prescriptive instructions are omitted from the fulfillment account, which records only the physical construction.

5. **vv. 6–9 — The Mercy Seat and Cherubim paralleling 25:17–20:** The mercy seat (kapporet) and its two cherubim are constructed exactly according to specification. The term "miqshah" (hammered work) is preserved, indicating that the cherubim were beaten from the same piece of gold as the kapporet — not cast separately and attached, but formed organically from the mercy seat itself. The phrase "porsey khenafayim lema'lah" (spreading wings upward) and "sokhekhim bekhanfeyhem al hakkapporet" (covering with their wings over the mercy seat) describe the spatial posture of the cherubim as a protective canopy over the throne-space. The phrase "ufeneyhem ish el-achiv" (and their faces each toward the other) — literally "a man toward his brother" — anthropomorphizes the cherubim's mutual orientation. The construction account omits the divine speech of 25:22 ("I will meet with you there and I will speak with you from above the mercy seat"), as this is a theological declaration rather than a construction specification.

6. **vv. 10–16 — The Table paralleling 25:23–29:** The table construction mirrors 25:23–29. Dimensions: two cubits length, one cubit width, one and a half cubits height. Gold overlay, gold rim (zer), handbreadth frame (misgeret), second gold rim around the frame, four gold rings at the four corners near the legs, acacia poles overlaid with gold — all reproduced precisely. Verse 16 lists the table vessels: plates (qe'arot), ladles (kappot), pitchers (qesvot), and bowls (menaqiyyot) "with which it would be poured out" (asher yussakh bahen), all of pure gold. The term "menaqiyyot" (bowls, from the root naqah, "to be clean/pure") denotes the libation bowls used for pouring drink offerings. The phrase "asher yussakh bahen" (with which it/they would be poured out) uses the Hophal imperfect of nasakh (to pour out, pour a libation), indicating the bowls' sacral function. The instruction of 25:30 ("you shall set the bread of the Presence on the table before Me continually") is omitted from the construction account, as it pertains to ongoing ritual use rather than fabrication.

7. **vv. 17–24 — The Lampstand paralleling 25:31–39:** The lampstand (menorah) construction is the most elaborate single object in the chapter. The term "miqshah" appears again, emphasizing that the entire lampstand — base, shaft, branches, cups, buds, and blossoms — was hammered from a single piece of pure gold. The botanical vocabulary is extensive: "gevi'im meshukqadim" (cups shaped like almond blossoms, from the root shaqad, "to watch/be alert" and also "almond"), "kaftarim" (buds, from a root suggesting a rounded knob or capital), and "perachim" (blossoms, from parach, "to bud, blossom, sprout"). The six branches (three per side) plus the central shaft yield seven lamp-positions, and v. 23 confirms seven lamps (nerot). The tongs (malqachayim, dual form, from the root laqach, "to take" — instruments for taking/grasping the wicks) and fire pans (machtot, from chatah, "to snatch up coals") are made of pure gold. The talent weight (kikkar, approximately 34 kilograms / 75 pounds) encompasses the lampstand and all its vessels (v. 24). The verb "kullahh echad miqshah" (all of it one hammered piece, v. 22) emphasizes the organic unity of the lampstand — a single mass of gold beaten into a seven-branched arboreal form.

8. **vv. 25–28 — The Incense Altar paralleling 30:1–5:** The incense altar (mizbach haqqetoret) construction follows the specifications of 30:1–5. Its dimensions — one cubit square, two cubits high — make it the smallest of the Tabernacle furnishings in footprint but the tallest relative to its base. The phrase "mimmennu hayu qarnotav" (from it were its horns) indicates that the four horns were not attached additions but were integral to the acacia-wood structure, carved or shaped from the same block. The gold overlay covers three surfaces: "gaggo" (its top/roof), "qirotav" (its walls), and "qarnotav" (its horns). The gold rim (zer) runs around the perimeter. The two rings positioned beneath the rim on opposite sides serve as holders (batim, literally "houses") for the carrying poles. The instruction text of 30:6–10 (the placement before the veil and the annual atonement ritual) is omitted from the construction account, as it pertains to liturgical function rather than fabrication.

9. **v. 29 — The Anointing Oil and Incense paralleling 30:22–38:** The final verse compresses the manufacture of two distinct sacred substances into a single sentence. The "shemen hammishchah qodesh" (the holy anointing oil) corresponds to the recipe of 30:23–25 (myrrh, cinnamon, calamus, cassia, olive oil). The "qetoret hassammim" (fragrant incense) corresponds to the recipe of 30:34–36 (stacte, onycha, galbanum, pure frankincense). Both are described as "ma'aseh roqeach" (the work of a perfumer/ointment-maker), indicating professional-grade compounding. The root r-q-ch denotes the specialized skill of spice blending and pharmaceutical preparation — the "roqeach" is the ancient equivalent of an apothecary. The word "tahor" (pure) modifying the incense indicates that the recipe was followed exactly, with no adulterating additions. The brevity of this verse (one verse summarizing what occupies seventeen verses in the instruction text) exemplifies the construction narrative's condensation of prescriptive material.

### Structural Notes

10. **Command-Fulfillment Architecture:** Exodus 37 is the fulfillment counterpart to the instructions of Exodus 25:10–40 (Ark, table, lampstand) and Exodus 30:1–10, 22–38 (incense altar, anointing oil, incense). The chapter follows the same sequence as the command text: Ark (vv. 1–9 // 25:10–22), table (vv. 10–16 // 25:23–30), lampstand (vv. 17–24 // 25:31–40), incense altar (vv. 25–28 // 30:1–10), anointing oil and incense (v. 29 // 30:22–38). The near-verbatim repetition constitutes the Priestly writer's demonstration of exact obedience. The shift from second-person imperative (the instruction) to third-person narrative (the fulfillment) transforms YHWH's word into Israel's deed. What God spoke on Sinai, Bezalel enacts in the camp. The literary architecture is itself theological: obedience is shown by correspondence, faithfulness is demonstrated by repetition.

11. **The Sequence of Sacred Objects — From Most Holy to Less Holy:** The order of construction in chapter 37 moves outward from the innermost sanctuary: the Ark and mercy seat (the Most Holy Place), the table and lampstand (the Holy Place), the incense altar (at the boundary of the Holy Place before the veil). This inside-out sequence mirrors the instruction order of chapter 25, which begins with the Ark — God's perspective, moving from His throne outward toward the people. The addition of the incense altar and sacred substances (vv. 25–29), which were instructed in chapter 30 rather than chapter 25, is integrated seamlessly into the construction narrative, restoring the spatial logic that was interrupted in the instruction text by the interposition of chapters 26–29 (Tabernacle structure, priestly vestments, consecration ritual).

12. **The Named and the Unnamed Craftsman:** The opening verse names Bezalel; thereafter, the subject is an unnamed "he." This literary pattern attributes the entire contents of chapter 37 to Bezalel's hand, creating a single-artisan framework for the most sacred furnishings. By contrast, the Tabernacle structure (chapter 36) uses the plural "they made" in many verses, suggesting collective labor. The distinction implies that while the structural fabric could be woven by many hands, the sacred furniture required the singular skill of the Spirit-filled master craftsman. The LXX preserves this distinction throughout.

### Theological Notes

13. **The Ark as the Theological Center:** The Ark stands first in the construction sequence because it is the theological center of the Tabernacle — the footstool of YHWH's invisible throne, the repository of the covenant, and the locus of atonement and divine speech. Its construction from acacia wood overlaid with gold inside and out embodies the Tabernacle's fundamental theology of presence: the incorruptible (gold) encloses and sanctifies the organic (wood), just as divine glory inhabits earthly material. The dual overlay — "from inside and from outside" — means that the Ark is gold wherever it can be perceived, whether from the perspective of the divine presence resting above or the human priest looking upon it. There is no hidden impurity; the sanctification is total.

14. **The Mercy Seat as Theological Convergence:** The kapporet, constructed from pure gold (without the wood-and-gold composite of the Ark itself), is the most materially pure object in the Tabernacle. It is the single point where three theological realities converge: (1) the stored law (the tablets inside the Ark below), (2) atoning mercy (the kapporet covering the law above), and (3) divine revelation (YHWH speaking from between the cherubim above the kapporet). This vertical axis — law beneath, mercy covering, revelation above — constitutes the spatial theology of Israel's redemption: the God who gave the law provides the cover for its transgression and speaks from above that cover. The construction of the kapporet "from the two ends" (mishshney qetsotav) of the same gold piece as the cherubim means that mercy and the guardians of divine holiness are one substance — atonement is not foreign to divine majesty but intrinsic to it.

15. **The Table and the Lampstand — Provision and Illumination:** The table bearing the bread of the Presence and the lampstand providing light represent the two fundamental modes of divine sustenance in the Holy Place: God feeds and God enlightens. The bread (lechem panim, "bread of faces") continually faces YHWH, representing Israel's perpetual presence before God and God's perpetual provision for Israel. The menorah, with its almond-blossom motif, is a stylized tree of light — an echo of Eden's tree of life, now placed within the sanctuary as a sign that communion with God restores what was lost in the garden. Both objects are overlaid with or made from pure gold, linking provision and illumination to divine purity.

16. **The Incense Altar — The Boundary of Approach:** The incense altar, positioned before the veil, marks the furthest point of regular priestly approach toward the Most Holy Place. The ascending incense represents prayer rising before YHWH (cf. Psalm 141:2, "Let my prayer be set before You as incense"). The altar's horns, integral to its structure ("from it were its horns"), suggest that the altar is not merely a platform for burning incense but an organic whole — a living form with projecting extremities, like the horns of the burnt offering altar. The horns receive the atoning blood on Yom Kippur (30:10), linking the incense altar to the sacrificial system. Its placement before the veil means that the fragrant cloud of incense fills the space between the priest and the divine presence, mediating the encounter with a screen of aromatic smoke.

17. **v. 29 — The Sacred Substances as the Culmination:** The chapter closes with the anointing oil and incense — substances rather than structures. This transition from solid objects to liquid and particulate preparations marks a shift from spatial theology (where God dwells) to sensory theology (what fills the space). The anointing oil consecrates persons and objects, transferring them from the common to the holy sphere. The incense fills the sacred space with fragrance, transforming the air itself into a medium of worship. Both substances are described as the work of a "roqeach" (perfumer), a craft that requires precise knowledge of proportions — the same precision that governs the dimensions of the furniture. In the Tabernacle's sensory world, sight (the lampstand's light), touch (the bread's weekly placement), and smell (the incense's perpetual fragrance) are all sanctified and directed toward YHWH.

18. **Formal Equivalence Methodology:** This translation maintains strict word-order fidelity to the Hebrew, preserves all waw-consecutive and waw-copulative conjunctions, retains the repetitive construction specifications without condensation or paraphrase, and provides transliterated glosses for each Hebrew term. The near-verbatim correspondence between this chapter and Exodus 25 / 30 is preserved in translation to maintain the command-fulfillment literary architecture. YHWH is transliterated throughout. Standard name forms (Bezalel) are used. Technical terms (kapporet, miqshah, menorah, roqeach) are translated with consistent English equivalents across chapters.

--- LOGOS
